[[File:安満宮山古墳出土 「吾作」銘斜縁二神二獣鏡.JPG|thumb|"Gosaku" inscribed diagonal-edged divine beast mirror excavated from the {{ill|Amamiyama Kofun|ja|安満宮山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amamiyama Kofun|qq=}}, on display at the {{ill|Imashirozuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imashirozuka Ancient History Museum|1=Imashirozuka Ancient History Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11376793}}]]

'''Diagonal-edged divine beast mirror''' (斜縁神獣鏡) is a type of [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirror]] characterized by a gently inward-sloping edge, belonging to the [[:en:Shinju-kyo|divine beast mirror]] family. These mirrors are predominantly excavated from {{ill|Early Kofun period|ja|古墳時代前期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Early Kofun period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Kofuns in Japan. Often considered a precursor to the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirror|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/triangular-rimmed divine beast mirror|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}}, the classification remains debated.

== Definition ==
Ryosuke Sasamori defines the diagonal-edged divine beast mirror with the following characteristics:{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2009|p=101-104}}
* The central decoration is divided by nipples, featuring concentric depictions of two gods and two or four beasts. The gods are accompanied by attendants.
* The inscription band often begins with "吾作明竟."
* The edge is steeper than flat rims but shallower than triangular edges.
* Diameter ranges from 10 to 18 cm.
* The outermost edge features a protruding boundary line.
As of 2009, 53 examples were confirmed, with 43 found in Japan. Their distribution ranges broadly from the Kinki region to Yamanashi and Ōita. Outside Japan, three were excavated in [[:en:Shandong|Shandong Province]], China, and five in [[:en:Lelang Commandery|Lelang Commandery]], on the Korean Peninsula.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2009|p=101-104}}{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2019|p=194-197}}

== Research History ==
Initially, diagonal-edged divine beast mirrors were not distinguished from triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors. In 1897, Miyake Yonekichi mentioned mirrors with "edges slightly inclined inward." However, Tomioka Kenzō treated the two as identical in 1920. In 1926, Gotō Moriichi noted edge cross-section differences and grouped them with triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2009|p=97-101}} Umewara Sueji separated them in 1921, describing their cross-section as "a mix of triangular and flat edges." In 1962, Umewara interpreted them as transitional forms between flat and triangular edges. Higuchi Takayasu coined the term "diagonal-edged divine beast mirror" in 1979, classifying them as a subtype of Later Han mirrors.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2009|p=97-101}}

Subsequently, efforts to typologically classify and date these mirrors were made by researchers such as Muramatsu Yosuke and Sasamori Ryosuke.{{sfn|Mabuchi Kazuki|2019|p=185}}

== Evaluation ==
Kazuki Mabuchi described diagonal-edged divine beast mirrors as one of the least conclusively evaluated mirror styles of the period.{{sfn|Mabuchi Kazuki|2019|p=185}}

Hidenori Okamura noted similarities between diagonal-edged divine beast mirrors and the Xu Province-based {{ill|upper-region divine beast mirrors|ja|上方系神獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/upper-region divine beast mirrors|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of the Later Han period, attributing their production to early 3rd-century Xu Province craftsmen. Some mirrors exhibit evidence of burial smashing, linking their introduction to Japan to the final [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]].{{sfn|Okamura Hidenori|1999|p=134-139}}{{sfn|Okamura Hidenori|2017|p=190-193}} Sasamori dates their production between 240 and 265 CE, categorizing them among mirrors combining traditional Han and innovative elements.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2019|p=197-199}}

Mabuchi and Morishita Shōji noted similarities to {{ill|Concentric Divine Beast Mirrors|ja|画文帯神獣鏡|lt=wide image-band deity and beast mirror|1=Shinju-kyo|2=ja|3=神獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Concentric Divine Beast Mirrors|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, particularly in inscription placement and depictions of Dongwanggong and Xiwangmu.{{sfn|Mabuchi Kazuki|2019|p=185}} Meanwhile, Nishikawa Toshikatsu classified them as a subtype of {{ill|human-dragon-tiger pictorial mirrors|ja|神人龍虎画像鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/human-dragon-tiger pictorial mirrors|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, attributing their origin to Lelang Commandery as "Lelang Mirrors."{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2009|p=97-101}}

Several scholars argue that diagonal-edged divine beast mirrors predate triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors. Fukunaga Shinya identified distinctive shared features, such as the "outer protruding line," suggesting earlier production.{{sfn|Fukunaga Shinya|1996|p=8-9}} Yang Jinping theorized that Xu Province craftsmen who migrated to Japan created triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors.{{sfn|Okamura Hidenori|2017|p=217-218}} Sasamori hypothesizes that mirrors presented to Himiko by Wei included diagonal-edged divine beast mirrors, inspiring domestic triangular-rimmed imitations.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2019|p=199-200}}

== Typological Classification ==
Diagonal-edged divine beast mirrors are divided into two groups based on stylistic differences. Muramatsu Yosuke categorized them into two periods using variations in double-wave patterns, while Sasamori linked these differences to distinct production groups.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2009|p=106-109}}

Sasamori estimates their production began in the early 3rd century (Phase 1) and continued until the mid-3rd century (Phase 2), ceasing before 265 CE.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2019|p=194-197}}

Sasamori estimates their production began in the early 3rd century (Phase 1) and continued until the mid-3rd century (Phase 2), ceasing before 265 CE.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2019|p=194-197}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q109594939]]
Wikidata name: shaen shinjū-kyō
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:佐味田宝塚古墳出土 斜縁二神二獣鏡-2.JPG|thumb|A diagonal-edged divine beast mirror excavated from the {{ill|Samida Takarazuka Kofun|en|Samida Takarazuka Kofun|WD=Q11383079|lt=Samieda Takarazuka Kofun}}]]
[[File:安満宮山古墳出土 「吾作」銘斜縁二神二獣鏡.JPG|thumb|"Gosaku" inscribed diagonal-edged divine beast mirror excavated from the {{ill|Amamiyama Kofun|ja|安満宮山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amamiyama Kofun|qq=}}, on display at the {{ill|Imashirozuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imashirozuka Ancient History Museum|1=Imashirozuka Ancient History Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11376793}}]]

'''Diagonal-edged divine beast mirror''' (斜縁神獣鏡) is a type of {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=bronze mirror}} characterized by a gently inward-sloping edge, belonging to the {{ill|Shinju-kyo|en|Shinju-kyo|WD=Q7497614|lt=divine beast mirror}} family. These mirrors are predominantly excavated from {{ill|Early Kofun period|ja|古墳時代前期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Early Kofun period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Kofuns in Japan. Often considered a precursor to the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirror|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/triangular-rimmed divine beast mirror|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}}, the classification remains debated.

== Definition ==
Ryosuke Sasamori defines the diagonal-edged divine beast mirror with the following characteristics:{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2009|p=101-104}}
* The central decoration is divided by nipples, featuring concentric depictions of two gods and two or four beasts. The gods are accompanied by attendants.
* The inscription band often begins with "吾作明竟."
* The edge is steeper than flat rims but shallower than triangular edges.
* Diameter ranges from 10 to 18 cm.
* The outermost edge features a protruding boundary line.
As of 2009, 53 examples were confirmed, with 43 found in Japan. Their distribution ranges broadly from the Kinki region to Yamanashi and Ōita. Outside Japan, three were excavated in {{ill|Shandong|en|Shandong|WD=Q43407|lt=Shandong Province}}, China, and five in {{ill|Lelang Commandery|en|Lelang Commandery|WD=Q701376|lt=Lelang Commandery}}, on the Korean Peninsula.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2009|p=101-104}}{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2019|p=194-197}}

== Research History ==
Initially, diagonal-edged divine beast mirrors were not distinguished from triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors. In 1897, Miyake Yonekichi mentioned mirrors with "edges slightly inclined inward." However, Tomioka Kenzō treated the two as identical in 1920. In 1926, Gotō Moriichi noted edge cross-section differences and grouped them with triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2009|p=97-101}} Umewara Sueji separated them in 1921, describing their cross-section as "a mix of triangular and flat edges." In 1962, Umewara interpreted them as transitional forms between flat and triangular edges. Higuchi Takayasu coined the term "diagonal-edged divine beast mirror" in 1979, classifying them as a subtype of Later Han mirrors.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2009|p=97-101}}

Subsequently, efforts to typologically classify and date these mirrors were made by researchers such as Muramatsu Yosuke and Sasamori Ryosuke.{{sfn|Mabuchi Kazuki|2019|p=185}}

== Evaluation ==
Kazuki Mabuchi described diagonal-edged divine beast mirrors as one of the least conclusively evaluated mirror styles of the period.{{sfn|Mabuchi Kazuki|2019|p=185}}

Hidenori Okamura noted similarities between diagonal-edged divine beast mirrors and the Xu Province-based {{ill|upper-region divine beast mirrors|ja|上方系神獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/upper-region divine beast mirrors|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of the Later Han period, attributing their production to early 3rd-century Xu Province craftsmen. Some mirrors exhibit evidence of burial smashing, linking their introduction to Japan to the final {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period}}.{{sfn|Okamura Hidenori|1999|p=134-139}}{{sfn|Okamura Hidenori|2017|p=190-193}} Sasamori dates their production between 240 and 265 CE, categorizing them among mirrors combining traditional Han and innovative elements.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2019|p=197-199}}

Mabuchi and Morishita Shōji noted similarities to {{ill|Concentric Divine Beast Mirrors|ja|画文帯神獣鏡|lt=wide image-band deity and beast mirror|1=Shinju-kyo|2=ja|3=神獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Concentric Divine Beast Mirrors|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, particularly in inscription placement and depictions of Dongwanggong and Xiwangmu.{{sfn|Mabuchi Kazuki|2019|p=185}} Meanwhile, Nishikawa Toshikatsu classified them as a subtype of {{ill|human-dragon-tiger pictorial mirrors|ja|神人龍虎画像鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/human-dragon-tiger pictorial mirrors|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, attributing their origin to Lelang Commandery as "Lelang Mirrors."{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2009|p=97-101}}

Several scholars argue that diagonal-edged divine beast mirrors predate triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors. Fukunaga Shinya identified distinctive shared features, such as the "outer protruding line," suggesting earlier production.{{sfn|Fukunaga Shinya|1996|p=8-9}} Yang Jinping theorized that Xu Province craftsmen who migrated to Japan created triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors.{{sfn|Okamura Hidenori|2017|p=217-218}} Sasamori hypothesizes that mirrors presented to Himiko by Wei included diagonal-edged divine beast mirrors, inspiring domestic triangular-rimmed imitations.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2019|p=199-200}}

== Typological Classification ==
Diagonal-edged divine beast mirrors are divided into two groups based on stylistic differences. Muramatsu Yosuke categorized them into two periods using variations in double-wave patterns, while Sasamori linked these differences to distinct production groups.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2009|p=106-109}}

Sasamori estimates their production began in the early 3rd century (Phase 1) and continued until the mid-3rd century (Phase 2), ceasing before 265 CE.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2019|p=194-197}}

Ryosuke Sasamori defines the diagonal-edged divine beast mirror with the following characteristics:{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2009|p=101-104}}
* The central decoration is divided by nipples, featuring concentric depictions of two gods and two or four beasts. The gods are accompanied by attendants.
* The inscription band often begins with "吾作明竟."
* The edge is steeper than flat rims but shallower than triangular edges.
* Diameter ranges from 10 to 18 cm.
* The outermost edge features a protruding boundary line.
As of 2009, 53 examples were confirmed, with 43 found in Japan. Their distribution ranges broadly from the Kinki region to Yamanashi and Ōita. Outside Japan, three were excavated in {{ill|Shandong|en|Shandong|WD=Q43407|lt=Shandong Province}}, China, and five in {{ill|Lelang Commandery|en|Lelang Commandery|WD=Q701376|lt=Lelang Commandery}}, on the Korean Peninsula.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2009|p=101-104}}{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2019|p=194-197}}

== Research History ==
Initially, diagonal-edged divine beast mirrors were not distinguished from triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors. In 1897, Miyake Yonekichi mentioned mirrors with "edges slightly inclined inward." However, Tomioka Kenzō treated the two as identical in 1920. In 1926, Gotō Moriichi noted edge cross-section differences and grouped them with triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2009|p=97-101}} Umewara Sueji separated them in 1921, describing their cross-section as "a mix of triangular and flat edges." In 1962, Umewara interpreted them as transitional forms between flat and triangular edges. Higuchi Takayasu coined the term "diagonal-edged divine beast mirror" in 1979, classifying them as a subtype of Later Han mirrors.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2009|p=97-101}}

Subsequently, efforts to typologically classify and date these mirrors were made by researchers such as Muramatsu Yosuke and Sasamori Ryosuke.{{sfn|Mabuchi Kazuki|2019|p=185}}

== Evaluation ==
Kazuki Mabuchi described diagonal-edged divine beast mirrors as one of the least conclusively evaluated mirror styles of the period.{{sfn|Mabuchi Kazuki|2019|p=185}}

Hidenori Okamura noted similarities between diagonal-edged divine beast mirrors and the Xu Province-based {{ill|upper-region divine beast mirrors|ja|上方系神獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/upper-region divine beast mirrors|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of the Later Han period, attributing their production to early 3rd-century Xu Province craftsmen. Some mirrors exhibit evidence of burial smashing, linking their introduction to Japan to the final {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period}}.{{sfn|Okamura Hidenori|1999|p=134-139}}{{sfn|Okamura Hidenori|2017|p=190-193}} Sasamori dates their production between 240 and 265 CE, categorizing them among mirrors combining traditional Han and innovative elements.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2019|p=197-199}}

Mabuchi and Morishita Shōji noted similarities to {{ill|Concentric Divine Beast Mirrors|ja|画文帯神獣鏡|lt=wide image-band deity and beast mirror|1=Shinju-kyo|2=ja|3=神獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Concentric Divine Beast Mirrors|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, particularly in inscription placement and depictions of Dongwanggong and Xiwangmu.{{sfn|Mabuchi Kazuki|2019|p=185}} Meanwhile, Nishikawa Toshikatsu classified them as a subtype of {{ill|human-dragon-tiger pictorial mirrors|ja|神人龍虎画像鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/human-dragon-tiger pictorial mirrors|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, attributing their origin to Lelang Commandery as "Lelang Mirrors."{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2009|p=97-101}}

Several scholars argue that diagonal-edged divine beast mirrors predate triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors. Fukunaga Shinya identified distinctive shared features, such as the "outer protruding line," suggesting earlier production.{{sfn|Fukunaga Shinya|1996|p=8-9}} Yang Jinping theorized that Xu Province craftsmen who migrated to Japan created triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors.{{sfn|Okamura Hidenori|2017|p=217-218}} Sasamori hypothesizes that mirrors presented to Himiko by Wei included diagonal-edged divine beast mirrors, inspiring domestic triangular-rimmed imitations.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2019|p=199-200}}

== Typological Classification ==
Diagonal-edged divine beast mirrors are divided into two groups based on stylistic differences. Muramatsu Yosuke categorized them into two periods using variations in double-wave patterns, while Sasamori linked these differences to distinct production groups.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2009|p=106-109}}

Sasamori estimates their production began in the early 3rd century (Phase 1) and continued until the mid-3rd century (Phase 2), ceasing before 265 CE.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2019|p=194-197}}

'''Diagonal-edged divine beast mirror''' (斜縁神獣鏡) is a type of {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}} characterized by a gently inward-sloping edge, belonging to the {{ill|Shinju-kyo|en|Shinju-kyo|WD=Q7497614|lt=divine beast mirror|1=Shinju-kyo}} family. These mirrors are predominantly excavated from {{ill|Early Kofun period|ja|古墳時代前期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Early Kofun period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Kofuns in Japan. Often considered a precursor to the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirror|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/triangular-rimmed divine beast mirror|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}}, the classification remains debated.

== Definition ==
Ryosuke Sasamori defines the diagonal-edged divine beast mirror with the following characteristics:{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2009|p=101-104}}

* The inscription band often begins with "吾作明竟."
* The edge is steeper than flat rims but shallower than triangular edges.
* Diameter ranges from 10 to 18 cm.
* The outermost edge features a protruding boundary line.
As of 2009, 53 examples were confirmed, with 43 found in Japan. Their distribution ranges broadly from the Kinki region to Yamanashi and Ōita. Higuchi Takayasu coined the term "diagonal-edged divine beast mirror" in 1979, classifying them as a subtype of Later Han mirrors.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2009|p=97-101}}

Subsequently, efforts to typologically classify and date these mirrors were made by researchers such as Muramatsu Yosuke and Sasamori Ryosuke.{{sfn|Mabuchi Kazuki|2019|p=185}}

Sasamori estimates their production began in the early 3rd century (Phase 1) and continued until the mid-3rd century (Phase 2), ceasing before 265 CE.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2019|p=194-197}}

[[File:佐味田宝塚古墳出土 斜縁二神二獣鏡-2.JPG|thumb|A diagonal-edged divine beast mirror excavated from the {{ill|Samida Takarazuka Kofun|en|Samida Takarazuka Kofun|WD=Q11383079|lt=Samieda Takarazuka Kofun|1=Samida Takarazuka Kofun}}]]
[[File:安満宮山古墳出土 「吾作」銘斜縁二神二獣鏡.JPG|thumb|"Gosaku" inscribed diagonal-edged divine beast mirror excavated from the {{ill|Amamiyama Kofun|ja|安満宮山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amamiyama Kofun|qq=}}, on display at the {{ill|Imashirozuka Ancient History Museum|ja|今城塚古代歴史館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imashirozuka Ancient History Museum|1=Imashirozuka Ancient History Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11376793}}]]

'''Diagonal-edged divine beast mirror''' (斜縁神獣鏡) is a type of {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}} characterized by a gently inward-sloping edge, belonging to the {{ill|Shinju-kyo|en|Shinju-kyo|WD=Q7497614|lt=divine beast mirror|1=Shinju-kyo}} family. These mirrors are predominantly excavated from {{ill|Early Kofun period|ja|古墳時代前期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Early Kofun period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Kofuns in Japan. Often considered a precursor to the {{ill|triangular-rimmed divine beast mirror|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/triangular-rimmed divine beast mirror|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}}, the classification remains debated.

== Definition ==
Ryosuke Sasamori defines the diagonal-edged divine beast mirror with the following characteristics:{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2009|p=101-104}}
* The central decoration is divided by nipples, featuring concentric depictions of two gods and two or four beasts. The gods are accompanied by attendants.
* The inscription band often begins with "吾作明竟."
* The edge is steeper than flat rims but shallower than triangular edges.
* Diameter ranges from 10 to 18 cm.
* The outermost edge features a protruding boundary line.
As of 2009, 53 examples were confirmed, with 43 found in Japan. Their distribution ranges broadly from the Kinki region to Yamanashi and Ōita. Outside Japan, three were excavated in {{ill|Shandong|en|Shandong|WD=Q43407|lt=Shandong Province|1=Shandong}}, China, and five in {{ill|Lelang Commandery|en|Lelang Commandery|WD=Q701376|lt=Lelang Commandery|1=Lelang Commandery}}, on the Korean Peninsula.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2009|p=101-104}}{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2019|p=194-197}}

== Research History ==
Initially, diagonal-edged divine beast mirrors were not distinguished from triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors. In 1897, Miyake Yonekichi mentioned mirrors with "edges slightly inclined inward." However, Tomioka Kenzō treated the two as identical in 1920. In 1926, Gotō Moriichi noted edge cross-section differences and grouped them with triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2009|p=97-101}} Umewara Sueji separated them in 1921, describing their cross-section as "a mix of triangular and flat edges." In 1962, Umewara interpreted them as transitional forms between flat and triangular edges. Higuchi Takayasu coined the term "diagonal-edged divine beast mirror" in 1979, classifying them as a subtype of Later Han mirrors.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2009|p=97-101}}

Subsequently, efforts to typologically classify and date these mirrors were made by researchers such as Muramatsu Yosuke and Sasamori Ryosuke.{{sfn|Mabuchi Kazuki|2019|p=185}}

== Evaluation ==
Kazuki Mabuchi described diagonal-edged divine beast mirrors as one of the least conclusively evaluated mirror styles of the period.{{sfn|Mabuchi Kazuki|2019|p=185}}

Hidenori Okamura noted similarities between diagonal-edged divine beast mirrors and the Xu Province-based {{ill|upper-region divine beast mirrors|ja|上方系神獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/upper-region divine beast mirrors|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of the Later Han period, attributing their production to early 3rd-century Xu Province craftsmen. Some mirrors exhibit evidence of burial smashing, linking their introduction to Japan to the final {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}}.{{sfn|Okamura Hidenori|1999|p=134-139}}{{sfn|Okamura Hidenori|2017|p=190-193}} Sasamori dates their production between 240 and 265 CE, categorizing them among mirrors combining traditional Han and innovative elements.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2019|p=197-199}}

Mabuchi and Morishita Shōji noted similarities to {{ill|Concentric Divine Beast Mirrors|ja|画文帯神獣鏡|lt=wide image-band deity and beast mirror|1=Shinju-kyo|2=ja|3=神獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Concentric Divine Beast Mirrors|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, particularly in inscription placement and depictions of Dongwanggong and Xiwangmu.{{sfn|Mabuchi Kazuki|2019|p=185}} Meanwhile, Nishikawa Toshikatsu classified them as a subtype of {{ill|human-dragon-tiger pictorial mirrors|ja|神人龍虎画像鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/human-dragon-tiger pictorial mirrors|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, attributing their origin to Lelang Commandery as "Lelang Mirrors."{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2009|p=97-101}}

Several scholars argue that diagonal-edged divine beast mirrors predate triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors. Fukunaga Shinya identified distinctive shared features, such as the "outer protruding line," suggesting earlier production.{{sfn|Fukunaga Shinya|1996|p=8-9}} Yang Jinping theorized that Xu Province craftsmen who migrated to Japan created triangular-rimmed divine beast mirrors.{{sfn|Okamura Hidenori|2017|p=217-218}} Sasamori hypothesizes that mirrors presented to Himiko by Wei included diagonal-edged divine beast mirrors, inspiring domestic triangular-rimmed imitations.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2019|p=199-200}}

== Typological Classification ==
Diagonal-edged divine beast mirrors are divided into two groups based on stylistic differences. Muramatsu Yosuke categorized them into two periods using variations in double-wave patterns, while Sasamori linked these differences to distinct production groups.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2009|p=106-109}}

Sasamori estimates their production began in the early 3rd century (Phase 1) and continued until the mid-3rd century (Phase 2), ceasing before 265 CE.{{sfn|Sasamori Ryosuke|2019|p=194-197}}
